text:
Drawing on a global network of scholars, this volume offers the first comprehensive study of the rise of referendums and the varying ways in which they are used in contemporary politics. Since the publication of Butler and Ranney’s landmark collection on direct democracy in 1978, referendums have become a ubiquitous phenomenon the world over, and this volume aims to fill a significant gap in the literature by offering a nuanced, scholarly understanding of the practice. In addition to contributions from scholars based in Europe and North America, this collection will benefit from the perspectives of scholars based in other parts of the world including Eastern Europe, Africa, Latin America, Asia and Oceania.

Through a combination of qualitative case studies and quantitative analysis, the contributions in this book examine how referendums are employed to constitutionalise change, to settle questions of sovereignty and to by-pass, complement or replace parliaments. Focusing on the government-led mechanisms and citizen-initiated processes that enable referendums, the chapters relating to the Swiss 'gold standard' of direct democracy as well as to other emerging and post-communist democracies shed light on the workings of direct democracy at different levels. The chapters on voting behaviour, campaign expenditure and policy outcomes in referendums seek to clarify whether or not direct democracy promotes political participation, and whether it is used as an attempt by the political executive to bolster its influence. With an appendix of a comprehensive list of national referendums held on constitutional issues from 1815 onwards, this volume is sure to make a significant contribution to our knowledge of the past and current dynamics of referendum democracy in the 21st century.
